name: Iphigenia
description: Iphigenia, daughter of King Agamemnon and Queen Clytemnestra, is a tragic figure in Greek mythology, primarily known for her near sacrifice at Aulis. As the Achaean fleet gathered to sail for Troy, adverse winds prevented their departure, and the prophet Calchas revealed that the goddess Artemis, angered by Agamemnon's offense against a sacred deer, demanded Iphigenia's sacrifice to appease her and allow the fleet to sail. Agamemnon initially resisted but, pressured by the army and his brother Menelaus, reluctantly agreed. He lured Iphigenia to Aulis under the pretense of marriage to Achilles. Upon discovering the true purpose, Iphigenia initially pleaded for her life, but ultimately, she accepted her fate, choosing to be sacrificed for the sake of Greece and the Trojan War. There are varying accounts of what happened next; some say Artemis, moved by her willingness, substituted a deer in her place and transported Iphigenia to Tauris to serve as a priestess. Others claim the sacrifice occurred as planned. In Tauris, she served Artemis, presiding over human sacrifices until her brother, Orestes, arrived seeking to steal a sacred statue. After recognizing each other, they escaped together, ending her exile. Iphigenia's story explores themes of duty, sacrifice, divine intervention, and the complexities of war and leadership, solidifying her place as a powerful and enduring figure in Greek tragedy.
subtitle: Daughter, Sacrifice, Priestess, and Heroine.
achievements: Serving as a priestess of Artemis

keyMyths.title: The Sacrifice at Aulis
keyMyths.description: To appease Artemis and allow the Greek fleet to sail to Troy, Iphigenia was lured to Aulis under false pretenses and nearly sacrificed by her father, Agamemnon. Some accounts say Artemis intervened, saving her at the last moment.

keyMyths.title: Iphigenia in Tauris
keyMyths.description: In some versions, Artemis rescued Iphigenia and transported her to Tauris, where she became a priestess presiding over human sacrifices. She later reunited with her brother Orestes, who had come to steal a sacred statue.

keyMyths.title: Escape from Tauris
keyMyths.description: Iphigenia, Orestes, and Pylades devised a plan to steal the statue of Artemis and escape from Tauris. They successfully navigated the dangers and returned to Greece, ending Iphigenia's exile.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: A Greek tragedy detailing Iphigenia's sacrifice.
sources.text: A Greek tragedy detailing Iphigenia's sacrifice.

sources.author: Euripides
